tree.heading: CHAPTER 1. DIAGNOSIS AND EVALUATION OF ANEMIA IN CKD
tree.content_preview: Anemia is a common complication in people with chronic kidney disease (CKD) and is associated with adverse clinical outcomes. The onset or progression of anemia in CKD may herald a new problem that is

children.heading: 1.1. Anemia in CKD
children.content_preview: An overview of anemia in CKD populations can be found in Figure 1, including its definition, prevalence, pathophysiology, and association with clinical outcomes. Each of these topics is discussed belo
ancestor_path: CHAPTER 1. DIAGNOSIS AND EVALUATION OF ANEMIA IN CKD

tree.heading: Definition of anemia in CKD
tree.content_preview: Anemia in adults is typically defined according to the thresholds from the World Health Organization (WHO): hemoglobin (Hb) <12 g/dl (120 g/l) for women, and <13 g/dl (130 g/l) for men 15 (Figur

tree.heading: Prevalence of anemia in CKD
tree.content_preview: The prevalence of anemia in CKD increases at lower levels of estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), reaching a prevalence of >50% at the advanced CKD (CKD G4-G5).4 The prevalence is dispropor

tree.heading: Pathophysiology of anemia in CKD
tree.content_preview: Anemia in CKD is frequently multifactorial. Common causes include relative erythropoietin (EPO) deficiency, shortened RBC survival, iron and other nutritional deficiencies (folate and vitamin B12), bl

tree.heading: Outcomes associated with anemia in CKD
tree.content_preview: Anemia in CKD is associated with several adverse cardiovascular, functional, and kidney outcomes. Studies have shown that anemia is associated with higher risks of coronary artery disease, heart failu

children.heading: 1.2. Iron deficiency in CKD

ancestor_path: Outcomes associated with anemia in CKD

ancestor_path: Outcomes associated with anemia in CKD
1.2. Iron deficiency in CKD

tree.heading: Definition of iron deficiency in CKD
tree.content_preview: Iron deficiency is typically defined based on 2 conventional indices, namely transferrin saturation (TSAT), reflecting iron availability in the circulation, and ferritin, reflecting iron storage. In p

tree.heading: Prevalence of iron deficiency in CKD
tree.content_preview: Iron deficiency is highly prevalent in people with CKD. The high prevalence is present in people with CKD not receiving dialysis, CKD G5HD, people receiving peritoneal dialysis (CKD G5PD), and KTRs. I

tree.heading: Pathophysiology of iron deficiency in CKD
tree.content_preview: Systemic iron deficiency implies a lack of adequate iron stores, which is mainly present due to blood loss, especially in the hemodialysis setting. 29 The frequent phlebotomies, blood remaining in the

tree.heading: Outcomes associated with iron deficiency in CKD
tree.content_preview: Many observational studies show that iron deficiency is associated with an increased risk of mortality and cardiovascular hospitalization in people with CKD. 28-30 In particular, iron deficiency, as c

children.heading: Practice Point 1.2.3: In people with CKD, anemia, and ferritin <45 µg/l, consider referral to gastroenterologists/gynecologists/urologists to identify the cause of blood loss.
children.content_preview: When a medical care provider identifies severe iron deficiency, defined by the American Gastroenterological Association (AGA) as ferritin <45 µg/l, or microcytic anemia (MCV <80 fl) in the absen
ancestor_path: Outcomes associated with iron deficiency in CKD

tree.heading: Research recommendations:
tree.content_preview: 1. Investigate the prevalence and health outcomes of iron deficiency in the absence of anemia. Important outcomes to assess include hard clinical endpoints such as mortality and MACE, patient-reported

tree.heading: CHAPTER 2. USE OF IRON TO TREAT IRON DEFICIENCY AND ANEMIA IN CKD

children.heading: Recommendation 2.1: In people with anemia and CKD treated with hemodialysis (CKD G5HD), we suggest initiating iron therapy if ferritin ≤500 ng/ml (≤500 µg/l) and TSAT ≤30% ( 2D ).
children.content_preview: This recommendation places high value on the potential benefits of iron for improving life expectancy and symptoms, reducing the required dose of ESAs, and reducing the need for RBC transfusion, and a
ancestor_path: CHAPTER 2. USE OF IRON TO TREAT IRON DEFICIENCY AND ANEMIA IN CKD

tree.heading: Oral or intravenous (i.v.) iron versus no iron
tree.content_preview: Nineteen studies have compared oral (8 RCTs 78-85 ) or i.v. iron (13 RCTs 78, 79, 86-97 , including 2 RCTs investigating dialysate iron 90, 91 ) versus placebo in people with CKD G5HD treated with ESA

tree.heading: Iron status thresholds to initiate iron therapy and treatment targets
tree.content_preview: Seven RCTs (11 reports) evaluated cut-off values of ferritin and TSAT at which to initiate iron therapy or to achieve as targets for iron therapy. 98-108 However, studies on longterm safety, cost-effe

tree.heading: Certainty of the evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of the evidence for iron therapy among people with CKD G5HD is very low (Supplementary Tables S4 and S5). The certainty of the evidence was graded to be very low for several crit

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: Although there has not been a formal assessment of patient values and preferences regarding iron, the Work Group believes most people with CKD G5HD would want iron if it prolonged life, reduced the ri

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: Iron supplementation seems to reduce the requirement for ESA therapy. Given the high cost of ESAs, appropriate use of iron in people with CKD would be expected to reduce overall costs.

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: It is difficult to precisely predict the effect iron will have on both Hb and iron status, the assessment of which requires repeated testing. This is particularly important given that results will dri

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: Several recent trials evaluated the benefits and harms of various treatment targets. By far the largest of those studies, PIVOTAL, found that compared with a reactive low-dose i.v. iron strategy, a pr

children.heading: Recommendation 2.2: In people with anemia and CKD G5HD in whom iron therapy is being initiated, we suggest using intravenous iron rather than oral iron ( 2D ).
children.content_preview: This recommendation places a high value on the benefits associated with more intensive administration of supplemental iron and the reduction in pill burden associated with i.v. iron. Most people recei
ancestor_path: Rationale

tree.heading: Key information
tree.content_preview:
Balance of benefits and harms

Eleven studies compared i.v. versus oral iron in 844 people with CKD G5HD treated with ESAs (Supplementary Table S6 and S7). 78, 79, 113, 115-123 Most were designed to e

tree.heading: Certainty of evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of the evidence for i.v. iron therapy compared with oral iron therapy among people with CKD G5HD is very low for all critical outcomes (Supplementary Table S6 and S7). The certai

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: The Work Group believes most people with CKD G5HD would prefer i.v. iron over oral iron as it can be administered during dialysis. Those at risk of or particularly worried about hypersensitivity react

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: Iron supplementation seems to reduce the requirement for ESA therapy. Given the high cost of ESAs, appropriate use of iron in people with CKD would be expected to reduce overall costs.

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: Oral iron is inexpensive and readily available in most parts of the world, which may not necessarily be the case for i.v. iron. Tables 3 and 4 outline the available oral and i.v. iron formulations, re

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: The Work Group felt that available data support the administration of i.v. iron to people with CKD treated with in-center HD, aiming to increase iron stores and probably reduce the ESA dose required a

children.heading: Practice Point 2.1: In people with CKD G5HD in whom iron therapy is being initiated, administer intravenous iron using a proactive approach to maintain stable iron status.
children.content_preview: Intravenous iron can be administered either proactively at regular intervals to maintain stable iron status or reactively when iron status test values fall below certain thresholds. The Work Group bel
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Recommendation 2.3: In people with anemia and CKD not receiving dialysis or treated with peritoneal dialysis (CKD G5PD), we suggest initiating iron if ( 2D ):
children.content_preview:
ferritin <100 ng/ml (<100 µg/l) and transferrin saturation (TSAT) <40%, or
ferritin ≥100 ng/ml (≥100 µg/l) and <300 ng/ml (<300 µg/l), and TSAT <25%.

This recommendation places a hi
ancestor_path: Rationale

tree.content_preview: Although there has not been a formal assessment of published evidence concerning values and preferences of people with CKD not receiving dialysis regarding iron, the Work Group believes that most woul

tree.content_preview: Iron supplementation is likely to reduce the requirement for ESA therapy. Given the high cost of ESAs, appropriate use of oral iron in people with anemia and CKD would be expected to reduce overall co

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: For people with anemia and CKD, iron supplementation is aimed at maintaining adequate iron reserves for erythropoiesis or stimulating an erythropoietic response, even in the absence of systemic iron d

tree.heading: Key information
tree.content_preview:
Balance of benefits and harms

Ten studies compared i.v. iron versus oral iron head-to-head in 1868 adults with CKD not receiving dialysis , ESAs, or HIF-PHIs; 150, 152-163 5 RCTs did so in in 800 adu

tree.heading: Certainty of the evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of the evidence for i.v. iron therapy compared with oral iron therapy among people with CKD not receiving HD is very low (Supplementary Table S12 and S13). The certainty of the e

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: The Work Group believes that patients will have varying preferences for i.v. or oral iron based on their health and mobility status. Oral options may be favored for their convenience, as they eliminat

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: Oral iron is inexpensive and readily available in most parts of the world. Intravenous iron requires facilities and personnel to allow administration which may be more costly to both the person with a

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: While oral iron is the more convenient option, adherence to oral iron may be lower. Several oral iron and i.v. formulations exist. Tables 4 and 5 outline the available and the recommended starting and

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: When compared with oral iron therapy, i.v. iron provided a small additional increase in Hb of about 0.3-0.5 g/dl (30-50 g/l), and increased ferritin and TSAT. However, i.v. iron may cause serious hype

children.heading: Practice Point 2.2: In people with CKD treated with iron, it is reasonable to withhold iron if ferritin ≥700 ng/ml (≥700 µg/l) or TSAT ≥40%.
children.content_preview: There is little doubt that iron treatment results in higher Hb in people with CKD. However, it is unknown at what levels of iron tests this erythropoietic effect is optimized. The KDIGO 2012 guideline
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.3: In people with CKD treated with oral iron, the choice between different formulations and dosing schedules is guided by cost, individual patient preference, tolerability, and efficacy.
children.content_preview: The various oral iron preparations have different bioavailability, dosing strategies, and gastrointestinal side effects (Table 3). If two or three times daily dosing causes gastrointestinal side effec
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.4: In people with CKD treated with intravenous iron, the choice between different formulations is guided by cost, individual preference, and recommended dosing schedules.
children.content_preview: Different formulations of i.v. iron differ in the maximum dose which can be administered at a single sitting and the rate of infusion (Table 4). 175 Some i.v. iron preparations, including ferric carbo
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.5: In people with CKD treated with iron, it is reasonable to test hemoglobin, ferritin, and TSAT every 3 months for those not receiving dialysis or CKD G5PD and every month for those with CKD G5HD.
children.content_preview: No clinical trials specifically determine the optimal frequency for testing iron status during iron treatment. Consequently, in line with previous guidelines, the Work Group agrees that it is reasonab
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.6: In people with CKD treated with iron, certain circumstances may warrant more frequent iron testing as shown in Table 5.
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.content_preview:
Initiation of or increase in dose of ESAs or HIF-PHIs
Episodes of known blood loss
Recent hospitalization
Important increase in ferritin or TSAT or overshooting target limit

ESA, erythropoietin-stimu
ancestor_path: Rationale
Practice Point 2.6: In people with CKD treated with iron, certain circumstances may warrant more frequent iron testing as shown in Table 5.

children.heading: Practice Point 2.7: Switch from oral to intravenous iron if there is an insufficient effect of an optimal oral regimen after 1 to 3 months.
children.content_preview: Oral iron is typically prescribed to provide approximately 200 mg of elemental iron daily, with most studies showing its effect on Hb concentration within 1 to 3 months. However, the desired effect ma
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.8: In people with CKD treated with iron, consider temporarily suspending iron therapy during systemic infection.
children.content_preview: Iron is essential for the growth and proliferation of most pathogens including many bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, and helminths. Iron also exerts subtle effects on immune function and host resp
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.9: In people with CKD treated with intravenous iron, considerations pertaining to hypersensitivity reactions to intravenous iron include the following:
children.content_preview:
Intravenous iron should only be administered if there is capability to manage acute hypersensitivity and hypotensive reactions,
Intravenous doses of iron should not exceed the maximum dose/administrat
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.10: The suggested management of reactions to intravenous iron is presented in Figure 7.
children.content_preview:
Figure 7 | Suggested management of reactions to intravenous (i.v.) iron. H1, type 1 histamine

Optimal clinical treatment of severe anaphylaxis includes adrenaline as an essential antianaphylactic dru
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 2.11: In people with CKD and profound iron deficiency (ferritin <30 µg/l and TSAT<20%) but no anemia, consider treatment with oral or intravenous iron.
children.content_preview: If profound iron deficiency (e.g., ferritin <30 µg/l and TSAT<20%) is present even in the absence of anemia, treatment with oral or i.v. iron could be considered in shared-decisionmaking, especi
ancestor_path: Rationale

tree.heading: Research recommendations
tree.content_preview:
Adequately powered pragmatic RCTs are needed to assess the benefits, harms and costs of:
o a proactive high dose i.v. iron regimen - such as used in the PIVOTAL trial - in people with CKD not receivin

tree.heading: CHAPTER 3. USE OF ESAs, HIF-PHIs, AND OTHER AGENTS TO TREAT ANEMIA IN CKD

children.heading: 3.1. Treatment initiation
children.content_preview: Practice Point 3.1.1: In people with anemia and CKD (whether treated with dialysis or not), the decision to use erythropoietin- stimulating agents (ESAs) or hypoxia-inducible factor-prolyl hydroxylase
ancestor_path: CHAPTER 3. USE OF ESAs, HIF-PHIs, AND OTHER AGENTS TO TREAT ANEMIA IN CKD

tree.heading: Key information
tree.content_preview:
Balance of benefits and harms

ESA increases Hb compared to placebo in people with anemia and CKD regardless of dialysis status. HIF-PHIs increase Hb compared to placebo in people with anemia and CKD

tree.heading: Certainty of evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of evidence comparing ESAs with HIF-PHIs in people with anemia and CKD not receiving dialysis is very low (Supplementary Tables S29-S37) for all of the critical outcomes reported

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: In the opinion of the Work Group, most well-informed people with anemia and CKD would choose to receive ESAs as first-line treatment for anemia, based on the long clinical experience with these agents

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: Direct costs of HIF-PHIs are evolving as these agents enter global markets, and the Work Group did not consider their costs or the relative costs of ESAs compared to HIF-PHIs or their administration w

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: This recommendation applies to adults of both sexes and all ethnicities with CKD G5D or CKD not receiving dialysis. There are insufficient data for efficacy and safety regarding the use of HIF-PHIs fo

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: ESAs and HIF-PHIs are both effective for treatment of anemia in adults with CKD G5D or CKD not receiving dialysis. ESAs are effective in children while HIF-PHIs have not been studied in children. Alth

children.heading: Practice Point 3.1.3: In people with anemia and CKD, HIF-PHIs should be avoided in those at increased risk of adverse events (Table 6).
children.content_preview: This practice point is based on theoretical concerns based on mechanism of action, preclinical experimental data, adverse event profiles from clinical trials with HIF-PHIs, and data from people with g
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: 3.2. ESA initiation
children.content_preview: Recommendation 3.2.1: In people with anemia and CKD G5D treated with hemodialysis (HD) or peritoneal dialysis (PD), we suggest initiation of ESA therapy when the Hb concentration is ≤9.0-10.0 g/dl (90
ancestor_path: Rationale

tree.heading: Balance of benefits and harms
tree.content_preview: As compared to placebo or standard of care, ESA therapy may reduce the risk of requiring RBC transfusions and increase QoL, especially when the pre-treatment Hb concentration is <9.0 g/dl (90 g/l).

tree.heading: Certainty of evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of evidence comparing the use of ESAs to reach a higher Hb target versus a lower Hb target in a CKD G5D population is very low (Supplementary Tables S40 and S41). The certainty o

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: The decision to initiate ESA therapy should balance the potential benefits of reducing anemia-related symptoms and RBC transfusions against the potential risks of harm. The increased risks of mortalit

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: Initiating treatment with ESA at the higher end of the range of Hb levels may lead to greater treatment-related costs and resource utilization, including costs for managing adverse events (e.g., vascu

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: Hb and blood pressure levels should be monitored in people who are treated with ESA or whenever there is a change in the dose of ESAs. The increase in Hb and blood pressure are generally reversible if

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: ESAs effectively raise Hb, which reduces the risk of RBC transfusions and increases QoL for people with CKD G5D and CKD not receiving dialysis and Hb concentrations <9.0 g/dl (<90 g/l). However,

tree.heading: Balance of benefits and harms
tree.content_preview: RCTs of ESA treatment among in people with CKD not receiving dialysis did not reveal a survival benefit or improvement in cardiovascular outcomes for higher versus lower Hb targets (Supplementary Tabl

tree.heading: Certainty of evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of evidence comparing the use of ESAs to reach a higher Hb target versus a lower Hb target in adults with CKD not receiving dialysis is very low (Supplementary Tables S43-S45). T

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: Choosing the Hb at which ESA should be initiated in this population must balance the critically important potential risks of stroke, other MACE outcomes , and worsening hypertension against the potent

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: ESA treatment-related costs and resource utilization, including costs for managing adverse cardiovascular events (e.g., stroke) may be higher with initiating ESA at higher Hb levels. However, the cost

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: People with anemia and CKD should be informed about risks and benefits associated with ESA prior to initiation of therapy. If a lower Hb threshold is chosen for ESA initiation, Hb may need to be monit

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: The Hb concentration at which ESA therapy is initiated in people with CKD not receiving dialysis should be individualized to balance the potential QoL benefits of ESA treatment among people with anemi

children.heading: 3.3. ESA maintenance therapy
children.content_preview:
Recommendation 3.3.1: In adults with anemia and CKD treated with an ESA, we recommend targeting a Hb level below 11.5 g/dl (115 g/l) ( 1D ).

This recommendation places a high value on avoiding the cr
ancestor_path: Rationale

tree.heading: Balance of benefits and harms
tree.content_preview: Although the systematic review from the ERT did not find a difference in mortality in people with CKD not receiving dialysis treated with ESA to target a high versus a low Hb, several adverse events a

tree.heading: Certainty of evidence
tree.content_preview: The overall certainty of evidence comparing the use of ESAs to maintain a higher Hb target versus a lower Hb target in adults with CKD not receiving dialysis is very low (Supplementary Tables S43-S47)

tree.heading: Values and preferences
tree.content_preview: In the opinion of the Work Group, most well-informed people with anemia and CKD not receiving dialysis or CKD G5D would choose not to receive ESA to maintain a Hb level of 11.5 g/dl [115 g/dl] or high

tree.heading: Resource use and costs
tree.content_preview: Maintaining a higher Hb would result in higher healthcare costs related to the cost of ESA drug, drug administration, and hospitalization for stroke and other adverse cardiovascular events without pot

tree.heading: Considerations for implementation
tree.content_preview: This recommendation applies to adults of both sexes and all ethnicities with CKD G5D or CKD not receiving dialysis with or without a kidney transplant.

tree.heading: Rationale
tree.content_preview: Maintaining a Hb higher than 11.5 g/dl (115 g/l) with ESA therapy does not improve survival in people with anemia and CKD G5D or CKD not receiving dialysis and may result in adverse cardiovascular out

children.heading: 3.4. ESA dosing, frequency, route of administration, and monitoring
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: 3.4.1. ESA dosing
children.content_preview: Practice Point 3.4.1.1: In people with anemia and CKD treated with ESA, the initial dose of ESA should be determined by the Hb concentration of the person, their body weight, and clinical circumstance
ancestor_path: Rationale
3.4. ESA dosing, frequency, route of administration, and monitoring

children.heading: Practice Point 3.4.1.3: In people with anemia and CKD treated with ESA, administer ESAs with the lowest dose possible which achieves and maintain treatment goals.
children.content_preview: High doses of ESA may contribute to the higher risk of stroke and other cardiovascular events associated with higher Hb targets in people with anemia and CKD treated with ESA. This was shown in the Tr
ancestor_path: Rationale

3.4.1. ESA dosing

children.heading: 3.4.2. ESA route of administration
children.content_preview: Practice Point 3.4.2.1: In adults and children with anemia and CKD G5HD treated with ESA, choose the ESA administration route (i.v. vs. subcutaneous) based on patient preferences, local practices, and
ancestor_path: Rationale
3.4. ESA dosing, frequency, route of administration, and monitoring

children.heading: 3.4.3. Frequency of administration and monitoring of ESAs
children.content_preview: Practice Point 3.4.3.1: In people with CKD G5 or CKD not receiving dialysis, individualize the frequency of administration of ESA based on patient preferences and type of ESA administered (Table 7).
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: 3.5. HIF-PHI treatment initiation and maintenance
children.content_preview:
Practice Point 3.5.1: In people with anemia and CKD, including those with ESA hyporesponsiveness, do not use ESAs and HIF-PHIs in combination.

No RCTs have investigated the efficacy or safety of comb
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: Practice Point 3.5.5: In people with anemia and CKD, do not escalate HIF-PHI doses beyond the recommended maximum dose.
children.content_preview: This practice point considers the possibility that, based on mechanism of action, higher HIF-PHI doses may result in adverse events.
ancestor_path: Rationale
3.5. HIF-PHI treatment initiation and maintenance

children.heading: 3.6. HIF-PHI monitoring
children.content_preview:
Practice Point 3.6.1: In people with anemia and CKD, when dosing HIF-PHIs, monitor the Hb levels 2-4 weeks after initiation or dose changes and subsequently, every 4 weeks during therapy.

This practi
ancestor_path: Rationale

children.heading: 3.7. ESA hyporesponsiveness
children.content_preview: Practice Point 3.7.1: In people with anemia and CKD G5D and CKD not receiving dialysis with initial or subsequent ESA hyporesponsiveness, identify and treat the underlying causes of ESA hyporesponsive

children.heading: Practice Point 3.7.4: In patients with CKD, anemia, and ESA hyporesponsiveness, if a desired erythropoietic response has not been achieved after 3-4 months of initiating a trial of HIF-PHI, discontinue treatment.
children.content_preview: No long-term studies evaluate the risks and benefits of HIF-PHI use in people with ESA hyporesponsiveness. The doses of HIF-PHI used in available studies done in this population tended to be higher th
ancestor_path: Hyporesponsiveness to ESA in anemia of CKD

children.heading: Practice Point 3.7.5: In people with anemia and CKD not receiving dialysis or with CKD G5D who have active malignancy, a recent cardiovascular event, or recent vascular thrombosis do not use HIF-PHI.

tree.content_preview: Conduct RCTs to investigate the use of ESA to reach a specific Hb target or compare ESA with placebo for critical (all-cause mortality, MACE) and important outcomes (QoL, fatigue, vascular access thro

tree.heading: CHAPTER 4. RED BLOOD CELL TRANSFUSION TO TREAT ANEMIA IN PEOPLE WITH CKD
tree.content_preview: Red blood cell transfusions are a treatment option for anemia in people with CKD. The choice between RBC transfusions and other anemia therapies depends on their relative benefits and harms, which var

children.heading: Practice Point 4.1: In people with anemia and CKD, use red blood cell (RBC) transfusion as part of a comprehensive treatment strategy, carefully weighing risks and benefits in a shared decision-making process.
children.content_preview: The decision to transfuse RBCs to people with anemia and CKD is often challenging. Healthcare providers must carefully balance the potential benefits and harms on a case-by-case basis, involving peopl
ancestor_path: CHAPTER 4. RED BLOOD CELL TRANSFUSION TO TREAT ANEMIA IN PEOPLE WITH CKD

tree.heading: Potential harms of RBC transfusions
tree.content_preview: Potential harms of RBC transfusions are infrequent and encompass transfusion errors, infections, transfusion-related acute lung injury [TRALI], transfusion-associated circulatory overload [TACO], hemo

children.heading: Practice Point 4.2: In people with anemia and CKD eligible for organ transplantation, avoid, when possible, RBC transfusions to minimize the risk of allosensitization.
children.content_preview: The risk of sensitization after RBC transfusion has probably decreased over time, at least partly due to changes in blood transfusion practices and the use of more precise methods to measure allosensi
ancestor_path: Potential harms of RBC transfusions

tree.heading: Effect of leukocyte-reduced RBC transfusions on allosensitization
tree.content_preview: Many countries and institutions have introduced universal pre- or post-storage leukocyte filtration. Leukocytes may be a contributor to, if not the cause of, a number of adverse consequences of RBC tr

tree.heading: Effect of allosensitization on time to transplantation and outcomes
tree.content_preview: Previously, allosensitization has been linked with longer wait times compared to nonallosensitized patients. 317 Data from the 2010 USRDS Annual Report suggested an increase in median wait time to tra

children.heading: Practice Point 4.3: In people with CKD and chronic anemia, consider that the benefits of RBC transfusions may outweigh its harms in people in whom:
children.content_preview:
ESA or HIF-PHI therapy is ineffective (e.g., hemoglobinopathies, bone marrow failure, ESA or HIF-PHI resistance)
ESA or HIF-PHI therapy is harmful (e.g., previous or current malignancy, previous strok
ancestor_path: Effect of allosensitization on time to transplantation and outcomes

children.heading: Practice Point 4.5: In people with CKD and acute anemia consider RBC transfusion when the benefits outweigh the risks, including:
children.content_preview:
When rapid correction of anemia is required to stabilize the patient's condition (e.g., acute hemorrhage, unstable coronary artery disease), and
When rapid preoperative Hb correction is required.

tree.heading: Research recommendations
tree.content_preview: There is a lack of RCTs on the use of RBC transfusions as a primary intervention in people with anemia and CKD. Given the logistical difficulties in conducting such trials, it is likely that observati

tree.heading: Aim
tree.content_preview: The aim of this project was to update the KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for Anemia in Chronic Kidney Disease . 151 The guideline development methods are described below.

tree.heading: Overview of the Process
tree.content_preview: This guideline adhered to international best practices for guideline development (Appendix B: Supplementary Table S2 and Table S3) 333, 334 and have been reported in accordance with the AGREE II repor

tree.heading: Commissioning of Work Group and ERT
tree.content_preview: KDIGO and the Co-Chairs assembled and engaged a Work Group with expertise in pediatric and adult nephrology, including both dialysis and transplant specialists; cardiology; hematology; clinical trials

tree.heading: METHODS FOR GUIDELINE DEVELOPMENT
tree.content_preview: extraction, risk of bias assessment, evidence synthesis and meta-analysis, grading the certainty of the evidence of each critical and important outcome, and grading the overall certainty of the eviden

tree.heading: Defining scope and topics and formulating key clinical questions
tree.content_preview: The KDIGO 2012 Anemia guideline was reviewed by the Co-Chairs to identify topics to be included in the 2025 guideline. Scoping reviews of these topics were conducted by the ERT to provide an overview

tree.heading: Literature searches and article selection
tree.content_preview: Searches for RCTs were conducted on PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL). The search strategies are provided in Appendix A: Supplementary Table Sl. Because

tree.heading: Data extraction
tree.content_preview: Data extraction, from studies and existing systematic reviews, was performed by a member and confirmed by a second member of the ERT. Any differences among members of the ERT were resolved through dis

tree.heading: Risk of bias of studies and systematic reviews
tree.content_preview: The Cochrane Risk of Bias tool was used to assess risk of bias for RCTs based on the randomization process, deviations from the intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of the outcome

tree.heading: Evidence synthesis and meta-analysis
tree.content_preview: Measures of treatment effect - For dichotomous outcomes, a pooled effect estimate was calculated of the relative risk between the trial arms of RCTs, with each study weighted by the inverse variance,

tree.heading: Grading the certainty of the evidence and the strength of a guideline recommendation
tree.content_preview: The certainty of evidence for each critical and important outcome was assessed by the ERT using the GRADE approach. 342, 343 For RCTs, the initial grade for the certainty of the evidence is considered

tree.heading: Summary of findings (SoF) tables
tree.content_preview: Summary of findings tables were developed using GRADEpro (https://www.gradepro.org/). The SoF tables include a description of the population, intervention, and comparator and, where applicable, the re

tree.heading: Updating and developing the recommendations
tree.content_preview: Recommendations from the KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for Anemia in Chronic Kidney Disease were considered in the context of new evidence by the Work Group Co-Chairs and Work Group members,

tree.heading: Grading the strength of the recommendations
tree.content_preview: The strength of a recommendation was classified by the Work Group as Level 1 or Level 2 (Table 15). The strength of a recommendation was determined by the balance of benefits and harms across all crit

tree.heading: Practice points
tree.content_preview: In addition to graded recommendations, KDIGO guidelines now include 'practice points' to help healthcare providers better evaluate and implement the guidance from the expert Work Group. Practice point

tree.heading: Format for guideline recommendations
tree.content_preview: Each guideline recommendation provides an assessment of the strength of the recommendation (Level 1, 'we recommend' or Level 2, 'we suggest') and the overall certainty of the evidence (A, B, C, D). Th
